Dave D asked me if I had done my Tony Robbins energy yells. And I told him Bill does the yells for both of us. So you missed that. He was out earlier in the hall doing his yells and his woe yeas or yewows or, you know, hand claps and all that stuff. So if you had gotten here earlier, you could have seen that. I said last night to the IBAs, I spent all day yesterday with a client, Harvey Zemmel, who's here from London. And all day long it occurred to me, you know, the proximity of doing a consulting day with somebody and then the next day teaching them what you were doing to them, you know, probably would have been better with a little time lapse, you know, gone by. So here we are and for starters, a reminder of what you are at, which you are not at is a session about any of the basics of either the consulting or the coaching business. Any that work their way in will be by accident or has part of an answer to a question, but I will limit that. So there's a certain amount of presumed knowledge in the room and if you lack it, you'll have to go get it after the fact backwards. So like I'm not going to spend much time on the 12 ways you can structure a coaching program and, you know, that sort of thing. This is largely about the handling of the client. Whether that, and it doesn't matter whether we're talking about the client as a member of a coaching program or the client as a one-on-one coaching or consulting relationship. I'll use the word client interchangeably and most of what we talk about applies to any and all of those circumstances. And so mostly we're going to be talking about the getting of, although most of that will be tomorrow, and the control of and the extracting of maximum money from the client and the keeping of and the multiplying of. And so that's what we're going to spend most of our time on. And some of it will be, oh, I don't know, emotionally uncomfortable for some of you, I am sure. Because I'm not going to take the time to, every time I talk about a tactic, I'm not going to take the time to make you feel ethically, morally, spiritually, and metaphysically healthy about it. I'll take the time to do that maybe two or three times during the entire day. So some of what we're going to be talking about is literally about how to, without them noticing it, pick their pockets. And if that is really troubling to you, you may have a tough day. There is a certain amount of, when I prepared this, there's a certain amount of what I call the world's longest beard problem, which is an old story about the guy with the world's longest beard. And they go to interview him up in the mountains of West Virginia, and the interviewer asks him, does he sleep with the beard under the covers or outside the covers? And he never got a good night's sleep again. So there's a certain amount of things that I long ago stopped doing consciously that I now do unconsciously. You know, I've trained myself to do them and do them all the time and never give them any thought. And I realize I'm actually now doing them what I don't need to do them anymore, and actually it's not necessarily congruent with my objectives, and I'm still doing it because I ingrained it. But it's sometimes hard to consciously back all that out and enunciate it in conscious instructions. I have done my best. But again, you will get more out of me if you ask me questions, because you may provoke something that I, it did not occur to me while I was preparing. You may have observed something about what I do or what Bill does or what Glaser Kennedy does that we are doing unconsciously, and you, and I may not mention it unless you ask about it.
